text:
In this study, a new organic hybrid molecule, 4-(((4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl)thio)methyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one (1), was successfully synthesized through the reaction of a thiophen-substituted triazole-thiol with a chloromethyl-substituted dimethylcoumarin. The chemical structure of the resulting compound was rigorously confirmed using microanalysis, IR, and multi-nuclear ($^{1}$H and $^{13}$C APT) NMR spectroscopy. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations at the B3LYP/cc-pVDZ level were employed to model the gas-phase geometry and electronic properties, identifying the specific reactive sites through molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) mapping and global reactivity descriptors. 

The pharmacological profile of the compound was assessed using in silico ADMET and toxicity predictions. The findings suggest that the hybrid possesses favorable gastrointestinal absorption characteristics, does not penetrate the blood-brain barrier, and falls into toxicity class 4 (LD$_{50}$ approximately 1 g/kg). Furthermore, the therapeutic potential of the compound against the SARS-CoV-2 virus was explored via molecular docking and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations across several essential viral proteins. The compound exhibited high binding affinity for all tested targets, outperforming several known antiviral agents in binding scores. Notably, it demonstrated the strongest interaction with the Papain-like protease (PLpro). MD simulations over 50 ns confirmed the stability of these protein-ligand complexes, particularly with Mpro, PLpro, and Nsp3, highlighting the hybrid's promise as a structural scaffold for future antiviral drug development.
